data.title: A Closer Look At Apollo Global Management (APO) Valuation After Recent Share Price Moves
data.description:

Recent performance snapshot

Apollo Global Management (APO) has drawn fresh attention after a recent move in its share price, with the stock last closing at $111.42 and showing mixed returns across different time frames.

See our latest analysis for Apollo Global Management.

The recent 1 month share price return of 6.52% stands against a 90 day share price decline of 23.03%, while the 5 year total shareholder return of 159.11% points to a much stronger longer term record. If you are comparing Apollo with other opportunities in fast changing areas of the market, this could be a good moment to scan 20 top founder-led companies

With APO trading at $111.42, alongside a 40% intrinsic discount measure and a sizeable gap to analyst targets, the key question now is simple: is this a genuine value opportunity, or is any future growth already fully priced in?

Price to earnings of 18.4x: Is it justified?

On a P/E of 18.4x at a last close of $111.42, Apollo Global Management currently trades at a higher earnings multiple than both its industry and peer group, even though the SWS DCF model indicates a 40.4% intrinsic discount to an estimated future cash flow value of $187.06.

The P/E ratio compares the current share price to earnings per share and is a quick way to see how much investors are paying for each dollar of earnings. For a diversified financial group like Apollo, which earns fees from asset management and returns from principal investments and retirement services, the P/E can reflect how the market is weighing the quality, stability and expected path of those earnings.

Here, the signals are mixed. On one hand, Apollo is described as having high quality earnings and earnings have grown strongly over the past 5 years, with forecasts pointing to annual profit growth of around 23.9% and earnings expected to grow significantly over the next 3 years. On the other hand, profit margins have compressed from 17.9% to 11%, earnings contracted by 24.4% over the past year and return on equity of 12.7% is tagged as low, with forecasts suggesting ROE of 18.7% in three years that is still framed as low by the model. This combination points to the market paying a premium multiple that bakes in solid profit growth expectations while looking through the recent margin and earnings setback. At the same time, the fair P/E estimate of 22.3x from the SWS fair ratio model sits well above the current 18.4x, which suggests the multiple could have room to move closer to that fair level if those earnings growth forecasts play out and quality is maintained.

Compared with the US diversified financials industry average P/E of 15.4x and a peer average of 13.6x, Apollo screens as expensive on a simple relative basis. The gap is not marginal; it is a clear premium that relies on the market continuing to pay up for the earnings profile described above and for the business mix outlined in its asset management, principal investing and retirement services segments, especially given the model flagged that revenue is expected to decline sharply over the next 3 years.

data.title: FactSet (FDS) Q2 EPS Softness Tests Bullish Margin Expansion Narrative
data.description:
FactSet Research Systems (FDS) has just posted Q2 2026 results with revenue of US$611.0 million and basic EPS of US$3.60, as investors weigh these figures against ongoing earnings expectations. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$570.7 million in Q2 2025 to US$611.0 million in Q2 2026, while basic EPS over the same quarters shifted from US$3.81 to US$3.60, presenting a picture of higher top line with changing earnings per share. With a trailing net profit margin of 24.5% and earnings forecasts pointing to mid single digit annual growth, the latest numbers put profitability and efficiency in focus for anyone tracking how the business is converting that revenue into bottom line results.

See our full analysis for FactSet Research Systems.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up with the big narratives around FactSet Research Systems, highlighting where the numbers support prevailing views and where they start to challenge them.

See what the community is saying about FactSet Research Systems

Advertisement

TTM margins hold steady at 24.5%

  • Over the last twelve months, FactSet generated US$2.4b in revenue and US$587.8 million in net income, which works out to a 24.5% net margin compared with 24.1% a year earlier.
  • What stands out against the bullish view is that while bullish analysts expect margins to rise toward about 26.1% over the next few years, the latest trailing figures show a smaller step up, with:
    • TTM net income moving from US$542.5 million to US$587.8 million alongside revenue moving from US$2.3b to US$2.4b, pointing to steady rather than sharply expanding profitability.
    • Forecast earnings growth of about 6.9% per year being compared to a 5 year history of 9.4% per year, which sets a higher bar for the bullish call on earnings momentum.

Stronger bulls argue that these steady margins and recurring revenues could still support their higher growth expectations and valuation case. They back that view with detailed earnings and margin forecasts in the 🐂 FactSet Research Systems Bull Case

Top line growth trails wider market

  • Revenue is forecast to rise about 5% per year compared with an estimated 10.3% per year for the broader US market, which means FactSet is expected to grow more slowly than many other companies even though it produced US$611.0 million this quarter and US$2.4b over the last year.
  • Bears focus on this slower top line profile, and the cautious narrative is that:
    • Forecast revenue growth of around 4.5% to 5.3% a year and slightly softer margin assumptions, such as a move from roughly 25.4% to about 24.7% to 25.1%, could leave earnings growth closer to mid single digits instead of the higher pace seen over the last five years.
    • Rising technology and content costs, combined with revenue growth that is below the broader US market rate, may limit operating leverage even if assets under service and product usage keep expanding.

Skeptics argue that this slower forecast growth is at the heart of the cautious case on the stock. They spell out how that view connects to their expectations for margins and earnings in the 🐻 FactSet Research Systems Bear Case

Valuation screens as relatively low

  • FactSet trades on a P/E of 13.7x, compared with 33x for the US Capital Markets industry and 24.8x for peers, while the provided DCF fair value of US$371.86 sits well above the current US$216.99 share price.
  • Consensus views this mix of lower multiples and slower expected growth as a trade off, where:
    • The stock sits below both the industry and peer P/E averages, and below the cited DCF fair value, at the same time as forecast earnings growth of about 6.9% per year and revenue growth of about 5% per year are more modest than the wider US market.
    • A 2.03% dividend yield and what is described as high earnings quality are weighed against the flagged high debt level, so investors are left balancing income, valuation signals and leverage risk rather than seeing a one sided story in the numbers.

Next Steps

For Astronics, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow stands at about $57.26 million. The DCF model used here is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, which starts with analyst estimates and then extends them further out. Analysts provide explicit forecasts up to 2027, with projected Free Cash Flow of $94.28 million in that year. Simply Wall St then extrapolates beyond those years, with the model pointing to projected Free Cash Flow of about $205.14 million in 2035.

Bringing all those projected cash flows back to today results in an estimated intrinsic value of around $88.50 per share. Compared with the current share price of US$66.73, the model suggests the stock trades at about a 24.6% discount, which implies Astronics screens as undervalued on this DCF view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Astronics is undervalued by 24.6%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 58 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: Astronics Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful yardstick because it links what you pay per share to the earnings that each share currently generates. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger growth or see lower risk, and a lower P/E when growth expectations are more modest or risks are higher.

Astronics currently trades on a P/E of 81.12x. That compares with an Aerospace & Defense industry average P/E of 35.12x and a peer group average of 27.98x, so the shares are pricing in a much richer earnings multiple than these broad benchmarks.

Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” for Astronics of 51.45x. This Fair Ratio is designed to go beyond simple peer or industry comparisons by folding in factors like earnings growth expectations, profit margins, company size and specific risk characteristics. Because it is tailored to the company rather than averaged across a group, it can be a more targeted way to think about what a reasonable P/E might look like.

Comparing Astronics actual P/E of 81.12x with the Fair Ratio of 51.45x suggests the shares are pricing in more optimism than this framework would imply.

Result: OVERVALUED

data.title: The Tactical Trio: One Pure Quantum Blueprint, One Annealing Progression, One Cash Engine Composition
data.description:
Quantum computing is moving from the lab into real-world use at the same time as investors are wrestling with higher energy costs, sticky inflation signals, and changing interest-rate expectations across major economies. While bond markets, central banks, and commodity prices keep shifting, this screener focuses on companies at the center of quantum hardware, software, and research. It offers exposure to a very specific long term technology trend rather than broad macro swings. In this article, you will see 3 of the most interesting stocks from the Quantum Computing Stocks screener and what sets each one apart.

Advertisement

IonQ (IONQ)

Overview: IonQ operates quantum computing systems that clients can access through major cloud platforms and its own services, while also working on quantum safe communications, detection systems, and specialized hardware, support, and consulting contracts. The company partners with institutions such as the University of Chicago and runs its headquarters out of College Park, Maryland.

Operations: IonQ currently generates about US$130 million in revenue from Computer Services, with around US$87 million from the United States, US$17 million from Switzerland, and US$26 million from other international markets.

Market Cap: US$9.7b

IonQ is one of the purest ways to get exposure to quantum computing, with all of its revenue tied to quantum related services and a customer base that includes major cloud platforms and research institutions. Recent contracts in Europe and Asia and US federal work on secure communications, together with guidance pointing to higher revenue in 2026, indicate that demand for its technology is spreading across governments and enterprises. At the same time, IonQ is still unprofitable, relies heavily on external funding, has seen shareholder dilution, and operates with a relatively new leadership team, so the story depends heavily on execution. The key issue for investors is how this mix of rapid commercial progress and high risk could affect the next phase of quantum adoption.

IonQ’s rapid commercial traction and pure quantum focus can look exciting, but the real story sits in how that growth case lines up with expectations in the analyst forecasts for IonQ , and one potential blind spot investors often miss

D-Wave Quantum (QBTS)

Overview: D-Wave Quantum builds and operates quantum computers that tackle complex optimization problems for commercial and government clients, offering access through its Leap cloud service, on premises Advantage systems, and software tools that help developers build and run quantum applications.

Operations: D-Wave Quantum generates about US$24.6 million in revenue from Internet Software & Services, supported by customers across markets including Germany, the United States, Japan, Canada, Switzerland, and other regions.

Market Cap: US$4.8b

D-Wave Quantum stands out for turning quantum annealing into real commercial products. More than 100 revenue generating customers use its QCaaS platform, hybrid solvers, and high value system sales to tackle scheduling, logistics, and portfolio optimization problems. At the same time, the company remains unprofitable, carries externally funded liabilities, and depends on relatively few large hardware deals alongside a young board. This raises questions about how quickly usage will ramp to cover rising R&D and operating costs. Analysts still expect revenue growth above 40% a year and see potential in its dual track annealing and gate model roadmap. The key tension is how those expectations line up with execution risk, cash usage, and the market’s view on quantum annealing versus rival approaches.

D-Wave’s effort to turn quantum annealing into real revenue is gaining attention. The more important story, however, lies in how its cash needs, customer concentration, and roadmap fit together in the 1 key reward and 4 important warning signs

Meta Platforms (META)

Overview: Meta Platforms runs some of the world’s largest social and messaging apps, including Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, Messenger, Threads and Meta AI. It is also expanding into virtual and augmented reality through its Reality Labs segment, which sells Meta Quest headsets and AI enabled wearables like Ray Ban Meta glasses.

Operations: Meta generates about US$201b in revenue, with around US$199b from its Family of Apps and about US$2.2b from Reality Labs.

Market Cap: US$1,356.8b

For a quantum themed screener, Meta is interesting because it combines a large cash generating social media and messaging business with investment in AI infrastructure, custom chips and AR or VR hardware that could benefit from future advances in compute. Analysts see a gap between the current share price and their assessments of value, while the company reports high profit margins and strong returns on equity alongside significant spending on data centers and AI models. At the same time, regulators are testing new legal theories around social media “addiction,” privacy and competition, and Meta is exposed to advertising cycles. How that mix of legal risk, capital intensive AI initiatives and the economics of the existing business interacts is a key consideration in evaluating the investment case.

Meta’s cash rich social platforms and substantial AI or hardware investments can be hard to piece together. See how that mix is shaping up in the analysis report for Meta Platforms
